detection.selector: (() => { const p = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!p || p.style.visibility === 'hidden') return false; const el = p.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); const t = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return c === 'rgb(255,112,112)' && t === 'red'; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.currStim !== 'undefined' && window.currStim && window.currStim.key_answer) return window.currStim.key_answer; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let stimColor; if (c === 'rgb(255,112,112)') stimColor = 'red'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,112,255)') stimColor = 'blue'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,255,112)') stimColor = 'green'; else return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let m; if (g >= 0 && g <= 2) m = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g >= 3 && g <= 5) m = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g >= 6 && g <= 8) m = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g >= 9 && g <= 11) m = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else if (g >= 12 && g <= 14) m = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; else m = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; return m[stimColor]; })()

detection.selector: (() => { const p = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!p || p.style.visibility === 'hidden') return false; const el = p.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); const t = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return c === 'rgb(112,112,255)' && t === 'blue'; })()

stimuli.description: Color word ('blue' or 'green') displayed in red ink (rgb(255,112,112)).

detection.selector: (() => { const p = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!p || p.style.visibility === 'hidden') return false; const el = p.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); const t = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return c === 'rgb(255,112,112)' && (t === 'blue' || t === 'green'); })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.currStim !== 'undefined' && window.currStim && window.currStim.key_answer) return window.currStim.key_answer; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let stimColor; if (c === 'rgb(255,112,112)') stimColor = 'red'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,112,255)') stimColor = 'blue'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,255,112)') stimColor = 'green'; else return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let m; if (g >= 0 && g <= 2) m = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g >= 3 && g <= 5) m = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g >= 6 && g <= 8) m = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g >= 9 && g <= 11) m = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else if (g >= 12 && g <= 14) m = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; else m = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; return m[stimColor]; })()

stimuli.description: Color word ('red' or 'green') displayed in blue ink (rgb(112,112,255)).

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim'); if (!el) return ''; const t = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const c = (getComputedStyle(el).color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let ink = 'unknown'; if (c === 'rgb(255,112,112)') ink = 'red'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,112,255)') ink = 'blue'; else if (c === 'rgb(112,255,112)') ink = 'green'; return 'word=' + t + ' ink=' + ink; })()

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian parameter estimates for the congruent (control) Stroop condition in young adults yielded mu values in the high-500 to mid-600 ms range, with the bulk of subject means falling between ~540 and ~640 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young healthy adults' ex-Gaussian mu for congruent (color-only/neutral) trials in color-naming Stroop fell around 510-580 ms in vocal-response variants; manual three-choice variants run somewhat slower (typically 580-640 ms).

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for control (congruent) Stroop trials in young adults clustered between ~55 and ~95 ms across subjects.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young-adult sigma for neutral/congruent Stroop trials averaged ~65-85 ms with appreciable inter-subject variability.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (the exponential right-tail component) for congruent/control Stroop trials in young adults ranged ~80-130 ms across subjects.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young-adult tau on neutral Stroop trials averaged ~90-120 ms.

congruent.rationale: Color-word Stroop congruent RTs in healthy young adults consistently fit ex-Gaussian with mu in the 580-620 ms range, sigma ~70-90 ms, and tau ~90-120 ms (Heathcote et al. 1991; Spieler et al. 2000). Three-choice manual response (slightly slower than 2-choice variants) and 1500 ms response window justify mu near the middle of that band.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop interference produced a mu shift on the order of 40-70 ms for incongruent versus congruent trials, placing incongruent mu in the ~600-700 ms range for young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Incongruent-trial mu in young adults exceeded neutral/congruent mu by approximately 50-80 ms, placing the incongruent estimate near ~620-700 ms in color-word manual variants.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M.; Hubner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian decomposition of color-word Stroop showed both a modest mu shift (~30-60 ms) and a larger tau shift on incongruent trials.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma on incongruent trials was modestly larger than congruent (typical young-adult values ~65-105 ms).

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M.; Hubner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent-condition sigma estimates clustered around ~70-100 ms in young adults; sigma differences across congruency were small relative to tau differences.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau was the principal locus of the Stroop effect: incongruent tau exceeded congruent tau by approximately 20-50 ms, placing incongruent young-adult tau at ~110-170 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Incongruent tau averaged ~115-150 ms in young adults, with a tau-driven Stroop interference signature.

citations.authors: Steinhauser, M.; Hubner, R.
citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau increases of ~25-45 ms on incongruent vs congruent trials reflect a long-RT-tail contribution to interference.

incongruent.rationale: Stroop interference is carried primarily by the tau component (right tail) rather than mu shift (Heathcote et al. 1991; Spieler et al. 2000; Steinhauser & Hubner 2009). Mu shifts ~50-70 ms relative to congruent; tau increases by ~25-35 ms; sigma changes modestly. Implied Stroop effect: ~80 ms mean difference, consistent with meta-analytic norms for color-word Stroop in young adults.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Trial-by-trial RT series in two-choice and simple-decision tasks exhibit low positive lag-1 autocorrelation (typically ~0.05-0.25) embedded within longer-range 1/f structure.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J.; Farrell, S.; Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: After detrending and accounting for short-range AR(1) structure, lag-1 phi for choice-RT series tends to fall in the ~0.10-0.20 range in young adults.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-by-trial RT series in speeded choice tasks show low-to-moderate positive lag-1 autocorrelation (phi ~0.1-0.2; Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers et al. 2004). Captures short-timescale arousal/attention persistence not absorbed by other mechanisms.

citations.authors: Lim, J.; Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Across short rested-state sessions (10-30 min) on speeded reaction-time tasks, mean RT drifts upward by ~10-30 ms in total, equivalent to ~0.05-0.30 ms per trial in 100-300-trial sessions.

citations.authors: Doran, S. M.; Van Dongen, H. P. A.; Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sustained attention performance during sleep deprivation
citations.quote: Time-on-task slowing in rested young adults on a 10-minute PVT averages well under 25 ms total.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across 120 test trials (3 blocks of 40) plus practice, healthy adults show small monotonic slowing on the order of 15-25 ms total (Lim & Dinges 2008 meta-analysis on simple/choice RT vigilance). 0.15 ms/trial yields ~18 ms drift across the session — conservative for a short Stroop.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: The 1/f component of choice-RT series contributes on the order of ~10-30 ms standard deviation on top of trial-level Gaussian/exponential structure.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C.; Holden, J. G.; Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Power-spectral analyses of speeded-response series show 1/f^alpha components whose amplitude corresponds to ~5-20 ms SD additive variability across sessions.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral exponents alpha for cognitive-task RT series cluster near 1.0 (pink noise), with typical individual estimates in the ~0.7-1.2 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J.; Farrell, S.; Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimates of alpha in choice-RT series most commonly fall between ~0.7 and ~1.1 after correcting for short-range autoregressive structure.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha noise documented in RT time series across choice paradigms including Stroop (Gilden 2001; Van Orden et al. 2003). Alpha near 1.0 (pink) is canonical; small SD added on top of the ex-Gaussian draws models slow drifts not captured by lag-1 AR.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W.; Verguts, T.
citations.title: Dissociating conflict adaptation from feature integration: A multiple regression approach
citations.quote: When analyzing sequential congruency effects, trials following errors are typically excluded because post-error slowing and feature-integration repetition effects can mimic or mask conflict adaptation.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C.; Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Researchers commonly exclude post-error trials from sequential-effect analyses because post-error slowing introduces a confound that cannot be attributed to conflict adaptation per se.

citations.authors: Gratton, G.; Coles, M. G. H.; Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The interaction of previous- and current-trial congruency on RT (the 'Gratton effect') produced ~25-45 ms reductions in incongruent-trial RT when preceded by an incongruent trial, with a complementary cost on switch-to-congruent trials.

citations.authors: Kerns, J. G.; Cohen, J. D.; MacDonald, A. W.; Cho, R. Y.; Stenger, V. A.; Carter, C. S.
citations.title: Anterior cingulate conflict monitoring and adjustments in control
citations.quote: In a color-word Stroop task, RT on incongruent trials was reduced by ~20-35 ms when the preceding trial was incongruent vs. congruent.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across published Stroop, Flanker, and Simon studies, congruency-sequence effect magnitudes typically fall in the 15-50 ms range for the iI vs cI contrast, with cC vs iC asymmetries of ~10-25 ms.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Sequential modulation of conflict in Stroop is robust (Gratton et al. 1992; Kerns et al. 2004; Egner 2007). Healthy young adults show ~30-45 ms interaction (iI faster than cI by 30-40 ms; cC faster than iC by ~20 ms). Magnitudes set in the middle of published ranges. skip_after_error=true because post-error sequential effects are confounded with post-error slowing (Notebaert & Verguts 2007); the post_event_slowing mechanism handles that separately.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C.; Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Across published choice-RT and conflict tasks, post-error slowing magnitudes cluster between approximately 20 and 80 ms, with a central tendency near 30-50 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction time on the trial immediately following an error is reliably longer than on trials following correct responses, by roughly 30-70 ms in self-paced choice-RT tasks.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W.; Houtman, F.; Van Opstal, F.; Gevers, W.; Fias, W.; Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes around 20-60 ms were observed across two-choice flanker and Stroop variants.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing in Stroop is well-documented at ~30-50 ms in healthy young adults (Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011 meta-analysis; Rabbitt 1966 origin; Notebaert et al. 2009). Uniform range 25-60 ms encompasses individual variability.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Speedup over the first few dozen trials of a novel speeded task follows a power-law decay; first-block-vs-asymptote differences in young adults commonly range from ~30 to ~120 ms depending on task complexity.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Practice effects in Stroop are modest after a dedicated practice block: incremental within-session speedups of ~40-90 ms across the first test block are typical and reach asymptote rapidly.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Asymptotic performance on simple speeded-decision tasks is typically reached within the first 1-3 blocks of ~30-60 trials following dedicated practice.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Stroop performance stabilizes after a single practice block plus ~1 test block in most adult samples.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Stroop block sizes in published manual-response studies typically range from ~24 to ~96 trials per block.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Power-law fits to per-trial speedup in young adults yield decay rates equivalent to ~2-8% per trial in the first block of speeded-decision tasks.

practice_effect.rationale: Within-session practice effects in Stroop are modest after the dedicated practice block (60-80 ms speedup over first test block, asymptoting by block 2; Logan 1988 instance theory; MacLeod 1991 review). 60 ms initial offset decaying by ~4% per trial reaches near-asymptote by block 2.

condition_repetition.rationale: Stimulus/condition repetition effects in Stroop are largely subsumed by lag1_pair_modulation (the Gratton effect). Enabling both would double-count. The literature does not document a separable repetition-priming effect on top of conflict adaptation in this paradigm.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement requires sustained low-event-rate monitoring (Mackworth tradition). A 120-trial speeded Stroop with attention checks does not produce reliable vigilance decrement above what fatigue_drift already captures (Warm et al. 2008).

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy young-adult variability for color-word Stroop: between-subject SD of mean RT ~80-100 ms (Hedge et al. 2018 reliability study). Stroop-effect SD ~25-35 ms (the effect is large but moderately variable individually). Sigma/tau scaling ±15% covers individual differences in distributional shape. Accuracy SD ~2-3% with floor at 85% (below that suggests strategy failure, not normal variation). Omission rates rarely exceed 5% given the 1500 ms response window.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of overall mean RT in color-word Stroop in undergraduate samples was ~80-110 ms across replications.

citations.authors: Pronk, T.; Wiers, R. W.; Molenkamp, B.; Murre, J.
citations.title: Mental chronometry in the pocket? Timing accuracy of web applications on touchscreen and keyboard devices
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean RT in online speeded tasks are comparable to lab estimates, typically ~60-110 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop interference effect (incongruent - congruent RT) in young-adult samples was ~25-40 ms; effects are large but moderately variable individually.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Stroop interference effects are typically large (means of 80-200 ms) but show appreciable individual variability across published manual-response samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A.; Popiel, S. J.; Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Individual-subject estimates of sigma and tau in Stroop varied by roughly ±15-25% around the group mean across young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H.; Balota, D. A.; Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Within young-adult samples, individual sigma and tau estimates spanned approximately ±20% around the group mean.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of accuracy on color-word Stroop in undergraduate samples was ~2-4% on congruent trials and ~3-5% on incongruent trials.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Reported between-subject accuracy SDs in healthy adult Stroop samples typically fall under 5 percentage points.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Omission rates in young-adult Stroop with 1.5-2.0 s response windows are typically well under 2%, with between-subject SDs under ~1%.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Participants with overall accuracy below ~80-85% in Stroop are commonly excluded as failing the task; ceiling is bounded at 1.0.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect
citations.quote: Healthy young adults typically perform color-word Stroop at 90-99% accuracy on incongruent trials and 95-100% on congruent trials.

citations.authors: Hedge, C.; Powell, G.; Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox
citations.quote: Omission rates in healthy young adults on speeded Stroop with response windows of ~1.5 s rarely exceed 5%; common exclusion thresholds sit at 5-10%.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stroop_rdoc' with 6 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 6 temporal effects.
Produced 48 citations across 24 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 25 parameters; 4 are high-sensitivity.
